Unable to return to work duties requiring arm elevation

Strength testing: 3/5 supraspinatus on empty can test

2 weeks of partial relief, then return to baseline symptoms
inadequate pain control, GI intolerance developed

No significant glenohumeral arthrosis.; MRI (01/22/2025): Full-thickness supraspinatus tear measuring 2.1 cm with retraction to the musculotendinous junction. Moderate subacromial bursitis. extraction.requested_procedure: Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, right shoulder — CPT 29827
extraction.surgical_approach_if_mentioned: Arthroscopic with mini-open rotator cuff repair

denial_risk_flags.label: Physical Therapy Duration Below Typical Threshold
denial_risk_flags.explanation: Physical therapy was documented for only 8 weeks. Many BCBS policies for CPT 29827 require a minimum of 6–12 weeks of formal PT, but reviewers may scrutinize whether 8 weeks is sufficient given the severity of the tear and the short relief window.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Add documentation clarifying that PT was maximally attempted and that the treating therapist concurred with surgical candidacy, or provide PT discharge summary confirming plateau of progress.

denial_risk_flags.label: Corticosteroid Injection Duration Not Documented
denial_risk_flags.explanation: The corticosteroid injection entry does not include a treatment duration or number of injections attempted. Payers may require documentation of at least one injection trial with a defined observation period before approving surgical intervention.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Amend the chart to specify the observation period following the 11/05/2024 injection and confirm whether a second injection was considered or contraindicated.

denial_risk_flags.label: Conservative Care Dates Contain Placeholders
denial_risk_flags.explanation: Treatment dates for physical therapy, NSAIDs, and the corticosteroid injection reference placeholder tokens (October 2024, 11/05/2024) rather than resolved calendar dates. Payers require specific, verifiable treatment dates to confirm adequate conservative care duration.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Ensure all placeholder dates (October 2024, 11/05/2024) are replaced with actual calendar dates prior to submission to establish a clear and auditable conservative care timeline.

denial_risk_flags.label: Imaging Date Contains Placeholder Token
denial_risk_flags.explanation: Both MRI and X-ray are dated 01/22/2025, which is a placeholder token. Payers require actual imaging dates to confirm the study was performed within an acceptable pre-authorization window.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Replace 01/22/2025 with the actual imaging date before submission to ensure the imaging is recognized as current and valid for the authorization request.

diagnosis_codes.note: M75.121 (full-thickness rotator cuff tear, right shoulder) is a clinically appropriate and direct medical necessity indication for CPT 29827 arthroscopic rotator cuff repair.

conservative_treatment_duration.note: 2 of 2 duration-eligible treatments have an explicit numeric duration (100%). Meets the 50% threshold.

surgical_approach.note: The chart explicitly documents 'Arthroscopic with mini-open rotator cuff repair' as the planned surgical approach, which is a specific and procedure-appropriate technique for CPT 29827.

cpt_code_valid.note: CPT 29827 is a recognized orthopedic surgical code.

letter:
July 3, 2026
Blue Cross Blue Shield
Prior Authorization Department

Re: Kim, Rachel | DOB: 04/15/1975 | Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair (CPT 29827) | M75.121, M75.111
Member ID: See attached insurance card
Patient: Kim, Rachel
Date of Birth: 04/15/1975
Procedure: Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair
CPT Code: 29827

Dear Prior Authorization Reviewer,

CLINICAL HISTORY AND PRESENTING COMPLAINT

This patient presents with an eight-month history of right shoulder pain and weakness following a work-related lifting injury, reporting pain rated 7/10 at rest and 9/10 with overhead activity. Range of motion assessment demonstrates active forward flexion limited to 95 degrees, active abduction to 80 degrees, internal rotation to 40 degrees, and external rotation to 55 degrees, with strength testing revealing 3/5 supraspinatus strength on empty can test and an ASES Score of 35.2 out of 100. The patient is unable to lift objects above shoulder height, cannot perform overhead reaching for daily tasks, experiences disturbed sleep due to shoulder pain, and is unable to return to work duties requiring arm elevation.

DIAGNOSIS

The patient carries confirmed diagnoses of complete rotator cuff tear of the right shoulder (M75.121) and incomplete rotator cuff tear of the right shoulder (M75.111), supported by clinical examination and advanced imaging as detailed below.

FUNCTIONAL LIMITATIONS

1. Unable to lift objects above shoulder height
2. Cannot perform overhead reaching for daily tasks
3. Disturbed sleep due to shoulder pain
4. Unable to return to work duties requiring arm elevation

CONSERVATIVE TREATMENT HISTORY

1. Physical Therapy (September through the conclusion of that course, 8 weeks): The patient underwent an eight-week course of physical therapy, resulting in minimal improvement with pain persisting throughout overhead activity.

2. Corticosteroid Injection — Kenalog 40mg: A corticosteroid injection was administered, providing two weeks of partial relief before symptoms returned to baseline.

3. NSAIDs — Meloxicam 15mg daily (July through the conclusion of that course, 3 months): The patient was maintained on meloxicam 15mg daily for three months; this regimen provided inadequate pain control, and GI intolerance subsequently developed, necessitating discontinuation.

REQUESTED PROCEDURE

Arthroscopic with mini-open rotator cuff repair, right shoulder (CPT 29827).

Imaging obtained confirms the structural basis for surgical intervention. X-ray demonstrates Grade II AC joint arthrosis with no calcific deposits and no significant glenohumeral arthrosis. MRI reveals a full-thickness supraspinatus tear measuring 2.1 cm with retraction to the musculotendinous junction, accompanied by moderate subacromial bursitis, with the subscapularis confirmed intact. These findings are consistent with the patient's clinical presentation and support the need for operative repair to restore rotator cuff integrity and prevent further tendon retraction and muscle atrophy.

The patient's BMI of 27.4 and ASA II classification have been factored into the perioperative surgical plan.

MEDICAL NECESSITY SUMMARY

Conservative management to date has included an eight-week course of physical therapy, a corticosteroid injection, and three months of NSAID therapy, none of which has provided durable relief or restored functional capacity. The structural severity of a 2.1 cm full-thickness supraspinatus tear with retraction, combined with an ASES Score of 35.2/100 and the documented functional deficits outlined above, establishes that continued non-operative management is unlikely to achieve meaningful recovery. Surgical intervention is medically necessary to restore the patient's ability to maintain activities of daily living, return to occupational function, and prevent progressive deterioration of the rotator cuff musculature. Authorization for CPT 29827 is respectfully requested.

Sincerely,
Michael Torres, MD
Summit Orthopedic Group
